data.title: From Next-Generation Mobility to Global Expansion: iCAUR Demonstrated Its Vision at Beijing Auto Show 2026
data.description:
BEIJING, April 25, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- The Beijing Auto Show 2026, which opened on April 24, marked a key moment for Chery Group’s iCAUR as the brand presented two major highlights: the ROBOX concept car and the right-hand-drive (RHD) version of the V27. Both were presented by Dr. Su Jun, CEO of iCAUR.

The ROBOX concept car represents iCAUR’s exploration of future mobility. Named from a combination of “ROBOT” and “BOX,” it adopts a future-classic design philosophy, combining timeless proportions with forward-looking design elements. It introduces a new design language that merges a streamlined OneBox silhouette with a high-clearance structure, balancing aerodynamic efficiency with off-road capability. Distinctive features such as split round and rectangular lighting, clean modular surfaces, and a reimagined cabin space create a clear technological identity.

Alongside it, the right-hand-drive V27 reflects iCAUR’s ongoing global expansion. The left-hand-drive version of the V27, positioned as a new energy boxy vehicle with classic design, has already gained strong market response in China and the Middle East. Its combination of distinctive styling and practical capability has contributed to its growing recognition among users in these markets.

Developed as a global model, the V27 meets five-star safety standards in both China and Europe while adapting to the requirements of right-hand-drive markets. Its introduction supports the brand’s expansion across Southeast Asia and Oceania, where diverse driving environments and user needs require localized solutions. The model reflects iCAUR’s ability to translate global standards into practical, market-relevant products.

Beyond its product lineup, iCAUR continues to build a global user ecosystem. In partnership with China National Geographic, the brand is launching “The Grand Tour,” a global exploration program designed to connect users with diverse landscapes and cultural experiences.

At the same time, reflecting the idea that each iCAUR is unique, the second season of the iCAUR Player Carnival invites users worldwide to participate in co-creation through vehicle customization projects, with selected works to be showcased at the SEMA Show. A wide range of customized versions of the V23 and V27 will also be displayed at the booth, highlighting the brand’s approach to personalization and user-driven design.

At the exhibition booth, the AIMOGA robot was featured as an interactive element, demonstrating iCAUR’s exploration of intelligent technologies beyond the vehicle. Through real-time interaction and scenario-based applications, it reflects the integration of mobility, AI, and user experience.

As iCAUR expands its presence across more than 40 countries and regions, supported by a growing network of global partners, the Beijing Auto Show marks another step in the brand’s global development.

About iCAUR

iCAUR is a new-energy vehicle brand created for global users, rooted in the philosophy that “Classic Never Fades.” By integrating timeless design with advanced technology and global standards, iCAUR aims to become the first choice for users seeking a new-energy SUV with enduring character and versatile capability.

Contact Person:Zeng Zhaoqing

Email: cengzhaoqing@mychery.com

Website: https://www.icaurglobal.com/

data.title: CEO of Italian grid operator Terna to depart in early May for Eni role
data.description:
Italian grid operator Terna said on Saturday that CEO Giuseppina Di Foggia, who is departing to become chair of the board of energy group Eni , will leave the company on May 5.

Terna also said that Di Foggia has agreed to waive the additional severance grants due for her position as General Manager, adding that she will be granted an end-of-mandate indemnity of 108,750 euros ($127,422.38) for her role as CEO.

Di Foggia had come under pressure from Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni to renounce the payout at a time when the government is struggling to find ways to help families and firms cope with higher energy prices.

In a separate statement last week, Terna said Di Foggia had "expressed her willingness to sign an agreement to give up her severance pay," adding that it would provide further details in due course "in full respect of the principles of corporate governance."

($1 = 0.8535 euros)

data.title: Republicans retool midterm strategy: Trump's policies, but less Trump
data.description:
Key points:
  • Republicans shift focus to local issues, play down Trump amid concerns over his approval ratings
  • Rising gas prices and Iran conflict could undermine Republican economic messaging
  • Some Republican strategists question optimism after Virginia redistricting loss

By Jacob Bogage, Nandita Bose and Matt Spetalnick

With U.S. gas prices up, President Donald Trump's approval ratings down and the Iran war dragging on, Republicans are recalibrating their blueprint ahead of November's midterm elections.

The strategy? Seek to tap Trump's turnout power without making the races a referendum on an increasingly unpopular president.

In a closed-door meeting this week with top conservative campaign officials, Trump’s political advisers – including White House chief of staff Susie Wiles, political chief James Blair and longtime pollster Tony Fabrizio – outlined a plan for candidates to promote Republicans’ tax cuts and inflation-fighting policies, according to four people familiar with the gathering.

But Republicans want to avoid making Trump himself the focus of the campaign, as strategists worry that his sagging political fortunes could hurt candidates in competitive congressional races. Trump's party faces an uphill battle to keep its House of Representatives majority, and a growing risk of losing control of the Senate.

Among some Republican operatives, concern is increasing that Trump’s presidency – and political clout – are running out of gas, according to three of the people, plus another seasoned Republican campaign source, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss private meetings and offer candid assessments.

Trump appears mired in a deadlock with Iran, with both military and diplomatic efforts falling far short of denuclearizing the Islamic Republic and reopening the Strait of Hormuz after two months of war. Rising gas prices – the national average is near $4 per gallon, according to AAA – threaten to neutralize new tax policies from Republicans’ “One Big Beautiful Bill Act,” the signature legislative achievement of Trump’s second term.

Only 36% of Americans approve of Trump's job performance, the lowest of his current term, a Reuters/Ipsos poll found. And many Americans, including some Republicans, have some concerns about the 79-year-old president's temperament and mental sharpness following a series of explosive outbursts.

"Democrats are going to try to nationalize the election and say we’re a rubber stamp for Trump," a Trumpworld political strategist told Reuters. "We have to break out of that and show race by race why we’re the better choice."

Inside the president's political operation, enthusiasm remains strong that Trump is an effective messenger. Kiersten Pels, national press secretary for the Republican National Committee, said that Trump would remain "the most powerful driver" of conservative voter turnout in the midterms, and that Republican candidates are eagerly seeking his endorsement.

White House spokeswoman Olivia Wales said Trump was the "unequivocal leader of the Republican party and he is committed to maintaining Republicans’ majority in Congress."

EMPHASIS ON LOCAL ISSUES, NOT TRUMP

Over coffee and pastries in the meeting on Monday held at what was once Trump’s luxury Washington hotel, now the Waldorf Astoria, Trump’s team asked guests to sign non-disclosure agreements, then predicted Republicans would win a redistricting election the next day in Virginia. The mood was optimistic, the people familiar with the gathering said.

Details of the meeting leaked immediately. A day later, Virginia voters approved the new congressional map Democrats drew to favor their party in November.

“If the people framing this approach are confident about Virginia and they get beat in Virginia, you have to question, are they overconfident about the whole package?” one of the people familiar with the meeting said.

Some Republican insiders are quick to point out that the midterm elections are months away, and that much can change before voters go to the polls. If armed hostilities with Iran slow, gas prices could fall and inflation could cool more broadly.

"The panic is people looking at things right now, but I think the key is to project where it could be over the summer, and it's still very fluid," said David McIntosh, president of the Trump-aligned Club for Growth.

Headed into the election cycle, Republicans planned to promote Trump as the party’s standard-bearer, and as the figure who, in his oft-repeated phrase, turned the U.S. into “the hottest country anywhere in the world.”

Wiles in December said Republicans would upend the traditional midterm playbook by putting Trump “on the ballot,” rather than keeping the sitting president at a distance.

Now, the people said, that plan is less attractive. Republicans will look to emphasize local issues rather than allegiance to the president, they added.

“The politics have changed,” said another of the people familiar with the meeting. “In January, nationalizing the race around him made some sense.

“Voters don’t feel the president is doing enough to make their lives cheaper, but they still believe Republicans want to do that,” the person said.

The Trumpworld strategist added that the Democratic Party's low popularity gives Republicans an effective foil with which to contrast policy ideas.

Trump’s faltering support, though, could give Democrats fertile ground to attach Republican candidates to the president’s shortcomings, making some conservative campaign operatives skeptical of the White House’s political approach.

After campaigning in 2024 as a critic of “stupid wars” and styling himself as a “peace president,” Trump is now overseeing the largest U.S. military operation since the 2003 invasion of Iraq.

Critics say Trump's administration showed little consideration of how Iran would respond to the joint U.S.-Israeli attack or the vast economic fallout, including an unprecedented global energy supply shock and the threat of a worldwide financial downturn.

Trump's decision on Tuesday to indefinitely extend what was originally a two-week ceasefire was widely viewed as a retreat, with Tehran maintaining its grip on the Strait of Hormuz and commitment to a nuclear program.

Aaron David Miller, a former Middle East negotiator for both Democratic and Republican administrations, said Iran believes it holds leverage with the vital oil shipment channel and can also endure more economic pain than Trump.

“The Iranians think Trump’s tolerance for an economic and political price is limited,” said Miller, an expert at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace. “They’re prepared to wait him out.”

data.title: Balchem Corporation: The Formulation-Embedded Compounder That Has Not Missed a Year
data.description:
The Business Nobody Categories Correctly

Somewhere inside a loaf of bread, a prenatal vitamin, or a batch of infant formula is an ingredient that was never meant to be noticed. It has no flavor of its own. It doesn't change the color of the product or appear on the front of the packaging. What it does is more precise than that, it controls exactly when and where an active compound releases inside a system, whether that system is a commercial oven, a human digestive tract, or a dairy cow's rumen. That is what Balchem Corporation has been doing, with growing sophistication, for more than five decades. It has been profitable in every year on record. It has increased its dividend in each of the past seventeen consecutive years. It carries a beta of 0.37 relative to the S&P 500, meaning it has historically absorbed less than half of the market's volatility in either direction. For an investor whose concern is not maximum return but dependable compounding without the stomach-dropping drawdowns that cyclical businesses produce, this financial fingerprint belongs on a very short list.

The company's core technology, microencapsulation, involves coating individual active ingredients in a protective lipid or polymer shell that dissolves under specific conditions, heat, moisture, pH change, or physical pressure, releasing the active precisely when and where it is intended to perform. For a bakery customer, that might mean a leavening acid that stays inert during mixing but activates at the right moment in the oven, preventing the texture problems and premature reactions that cheaper unencapsulated alternatives cause. For a pharmaceutical customer, it might mean a mineral or nutrient that survives stomach acid intact and releases in the small intestine. For a livestock producer, it means a choline supplement that bypasses rumen degradation entirely and reaches the bloodstream in usable form.

The result of all of this is a business that looks, from the outside, like a specialty chemicals company. It has SIC codes, manufacturing plants, raw material inputs, and industrial customers. It shows up in sector screeners alongside businesses that make polymer coatings and industrial solvents. That categorization creates a useful analytical starting point, because the gap between how the business is categorized and what its economics actually look like is precisely where the investment case begins. Not as a mispricing argument. As an explanation for why a business with 20% operating margins, a 17-year unbroken dividend record, and FCF per share compounding at approximately 10% annually for a decade keeps getting evaluated against peers whose economics bear no resemblance to its own.

What Balchem actually operates is closer to a formulation services platform. When a food manufacturer develops a new product using one of its encapsulated solutions, BakeShure for leavening control, VitaShure for nutrient delivery, MeatShure for preservation, that ingredient is not purchased off a commodity shelf and swapped in by a procurement team. It is engineered into the product's recipe. Balchem's technical team works directly with the customer's product developers to calibrate release profiles, test stability under the customer's specific processing conditions, and qualify the ingredient within the final formulation. That process takes months. The resulting relationship is not a vendor relationship. It is a formulation dependency.

This distinction has real economic consequences, and they compound over time. A customer who has spent six months working with Balchem to optimize a nutrient delivery system in a supplement line does not change suppliers because a competitor offers a marginally lower price per kilogram. The cost of switching is not the price difference, it is the reformulation work, the shelf-life retesting, the regulatory documentation, and the production line recalibration that comes with it. In categories where finished product quality is tightly regulated, that barrier is even higher. The business generates recurring, reorder-driven revenue not because customers are locked in by contract, but because they are locked in by the effort required to leave.

That is a different economic structure than a chemicals supplier. And yet the market has not fully reflected that difference in how it prices the business, which is where the investment case for an owner begins.

Where the Lock-In Actually Lives

The obvious answer to why Balchem retains its customers is technology, microencapsulation is proprietary, complex, and not easily replicated at commercial scale. That is true, but it is also the answer any analyst would give after reading the first page of the annual report. The more interesting answer sits one level deeper, in what happens after the technology has already been deployed inside a customer's product.

Once a food manufacturer, pharmaceutical company, or infant formula producer has finalized a product formulation containing one of Balchem's encapsulated ingredients, that ingredient is no longer just a purchased input. It is part of a tested, validated, and in many cases regulatorily documented formula. Changing it requires reopening the formulation entirely. For a bakery customer using BakeShure encapsulated leavening acids, that might mean weeks of bench testing and line trials. For a pharmaceutical company using an encapsulated mineral in a finished dosage form, it means revalidating stability and bioavailability data. For an infant formula manufacturer, where choline chloride is mandated by the FDA as a required nutrient, changing a qualified choline supplier means resubmitting documentation to demonstrate that the replacement ingredient meets the same nutritional and purity standards in the final product. None of those processes happen quickly, and none happen cheaply.

The switching cost structure varies meaningfully across the three segments. In Human Nutrition and Health, which represented approximately 64% of full-year 2025 revenue, the barriers are highest. Products in this segment serve pharmaceutical customers, prenatal supplement manufacturers, and infant formula producers, categories where finished product quality is subject to FDA oversight and where supplier changes trigger substantive internal review processes. The longer a customer has used a Balchem-qualified ingredient in a product already on the market, the more expensive the decision to replace it becomes.

Animal Nutrition and Health, at roughly 22% of 2025 revenue, carries different and more mixed economics. Choline chloride for poultry and swine feed operates in a more price-competitive environment than encapsulated human nutrition ingredients, and the switching friction, while present, is lower than in regulated pharmaceutical categories. This segment is where competitive pressure from lower-cost imports has historically been most visible, and it is precisely why the European Commission's action in December 2025 matters structurally, not just commercially.

After a 14-month investigation, the European Commission on December 19, 2025 imposed definitive anti-dumping duties of between 90% and 115.9% on choline chloride imports from China into the EU, effective for five years with the possibility of renewal. Balchem, which manufactures choline chloride at EU facilities certified to human nutrition-grade standards, was among the two producers that filed the original complaint in September 2024 alongside Taminco BV. The ruling, published under Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2589, confirmed that Chinese producers had been selling into the EU at prices that caused material injury to domestic production. For an owner of this business, the significance goes beyond near-term pricing relief. It signals that EU regulators viewed the domestic choline supply chain as strategically important enough to protect, a recognition that the supply chain dependencies embedded in the EU food and feed system justify trade intervention at scale. That is a regulatory tailwind for the segment's European operations that will persist for at least five years.

The Specialty Products segment, at roughly 14% of 2025 revenue, presents the most infrastructure-like economics of the three. Balchem supplies ethylene oxide, a sterilant gas used to sterilize approximately 50% of all medical devices sold in the United States, to healthcare facilities and contract sterilization companies. Ethylene oxide remains the only method capable of safely sterilizing virtually all medical device materials, including heat-sensitive plastics, elastomers, and battery-containing devices, a fact that the FDA has explicitly acknowledged in its own device sterilization guidance. When a medical device manufacturer selects an ethylene oxide supplier and qualifies that supplier through its sterilization validation process, changing suppliers is not a procurement decision. It triggers revalidation of sterilization cycle parameters, which in many cases requires resubmission of sterility data for a 510(k) cleared device. The FDA's own transitional enforcement guidance issued in November 2024 on facility changes for Class III devices illustrates just how tightly the regulatory framework controls the ability to change sterilization processes midstream.

Taken together, the switching cost profile across these three segments is not uniform, and that non-uniformity is part of what the market misses when it applies a single specialty chemicals multiple to the whole business. The regulated categories carry barriers that compound over time. The commodity-adjacent categories carry barriers that are more price-sensitive but now structurally supported by trade measures. And the infrastructure segment carries barriers that are, in the most direct sense, written into law.

Three Segments, Three Different Stories

The headline number from 2025, $1.037 billion in revenue, the first time the company has crossed that threshold in its history, is worth pausing on, not because of the round number, but because of what sits underneath it. Three segments with genuinely different economic profiles reached that milestone together, each growing simultaneously, which is not something this business does every year. Understanding why they grew, and how differently they earn, is where the investment case becomes specific rather than general.

Human Nutrition and Health is the engine. At approximately $659 million in full-year 2025 revenue, it represents roughly 64% of the total and is the segment where Balchem's formulation-embedded positioning is most clearly expressed in the financials. Q4 2025 sales of $166.1 million were up 12.7% year over year, driven by growth in both the nutrients business and the food ingredients and solutions business simultaneously, not a single product line carrying an otherwise flat segment, but broad-based momentum across the portfolio. The segment's Q4 operating margin came in at approximately 22%, reflecting the pricing power that embedded formulation relationships enable. These are not customers buying on spot market terms. They are customers whose product recipes already contain a Balchem ingredient, reordering because the alternative is a reformulation process they have no incentive to start. The segment also carries the highest proportion of pharmaceutical and regulated nutrition customers, where the switching barriers described earlier are strongest. That combination, recurring reorder patterns, regulated customer base, and above-average operating margins, makes this segment the primary reason any serious owner looks at this business at all.

Animal Nutrition and Health is a fundamentally different story. At approximately $231 million in full-year 2025 revenue, representing 22% of the total, this segment operates in markets where price competition is more direct and where the economics are more sensitive to commodity input costs and livestock industry cycles. Q4 2025 operating earnings of $6.2 million on $61.2 million in sales imply a margin of roughly 10%, meaningful, but less than half the margin profile of the nutrition business. The segment did grow 4.9% in Q4 and showed improvement across both ruminant and monogastric species markets, and the EU anti-dumping measures on Chinese choline chloride now provide a structural floor for the European portion of this business that did not exist a year ago. But an owner needs to be clear-eyed about what this segment is: a solid, improving, but inherently more commodity-adjacent business that pulls the blended company margin below what the nutrition platform would generate on its own.

Specialty Products, at approximately $142 million in 2025 revenue and roughly 14% of the total, is the segment that gets the least analytical attention and perhaps deserves the most. It operates two businesses with very different end markets, performance gases, primarily ethylene oxide for medical device sterilization, and plant nutrition, supplying chelated minerals to agricultural customers globally. The performance gases business earns economics that are closer in character to a regulated infrastructure asset than anything else in the portfolio. Ethylene oxide is not a commodity sterilant that hospital systems and contract sterilizers evaluate on quarterly pricing. It is a process-qualified input embedded in validated sterilization cycles for specific devices. The Q1 2025 Specialty Products segment showed operating earnings of $9.6 million on $33.3 million in sales, an operating margin of approximately 29%, the strongest margin profile of any segment in that quarter. That is not an accident. It reflects the captive nature of the customer relationships in performance gases and the pricing leverage that comes from being embedded in a medically regulated process that cannot easily be changed.

Taken together, the three segments produce a company that generated $274.9 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2025, up 9.8% from the prior year, on revenue that grew 8.8%. Free cash flow reached a record $173.6 million, up from $162.6 million in 2024, with cash conversion from operations of approximately 105% of net income, a figure that reflects the low capital intensity of the formulation and gases businesses relative to their earnings. The company ended 2025 with net debt of approximately $127 million against adjusted EBITDA of $274.9 million, a ratio below 0.5 times, a balance sheet conservative enough to support the $36 million microencapsulation facility currently under construction in Orange County, New York, the 10.3% dividend increase declared in December 2025, and continued share repurchases, simultaneously, without financial stress.

The market applies a single blended multiple to all three of these businesses. That multiple reflects neither the recurring, margin-rich economics of Human Nutrition and Health, nor the infrastructure-adjacent pricing power of Specialty Products, nor the improving but more cyclical profile of Animal Nutrition and Health. It is an average, and averages, in businesses where the underlying economics are this differentiated, are rarely the right lens.

What the Per-Share Reality Actually Looks Like

The segment breakdown tells you what kind of business this is. The per-share numbers tell you whether that business has actually been working for its owners. Those are two different questions, and in cases where a company grows revenue steadily but dilutes shareholders or inflates earnings through tax rate compression, the answer to the second question can undermine the first. With Balchem, the two questions point in the same direction, but only after you do the work to confirm it.

Start with the earnings trajectory. Full-year 2025 GAAP diluted EPS came in at $4.75, up from $3.93 in 2024, a 20.9% increase. Adjusted diluted EPS reached a record $5.15, up from $4.37 the prior year, representing a 17.8% increase. These are strong numbers, and the first instinct for any careful owner should be to ask whether they are clean. Two adjustments deserve scrutiny before accepting them at face value: the effective tax rate and the share count.

On tax rates, the 2025 story is straightforward. The effective tax rate for the full year came in at approximately 22.2%, calculated directly from $44.2 million in income tax expense against $199.0 million in pre-tax earnings. For 2024, the equivalent rate was approximately 22.8%. Those figures are not meaningfully different, which means the per-share earnings growth in 2025 was not manufactured by a declining tax rate. The operating leverage is real. Compare this to the Helios Technologies situation, where a multi-year decline in the effective tax rate from the low 30s to the low 20s quietly inflated reported per-share progress without reflecting genuine operational improvement. Balchem's tax rate has been stable in the low-to-mid 20% range for several years, which means the 17.8% adjusted EPS growth in 2025 came from the business, not from the tax line.

On share count, the picture is equally clean. Diluted shares used in the 2025 full-year EPS calculation were 32.6 million, compared to 32.7 million in 2024 and 32.7 million in 2021. Going back further, the diluted share count has been range-bound between approximately 32.5 million and 32.8 million for the better part of a decade, an unusually stable figure for a company that has made several acquisitions during that period. The stability reflects a management team that has consistently used equity-based acquisitions with restraint and directed free cash flow toward repurchases rather than allowing creeping dilution. In Q4 2025 alone, the company repurchased $53.6 million of its own stock. That is not a token buyback programm, it is a meaningful reduction in the share count at a moment when management clearly viewed the current price as a reasonable use of capital.

The FCF per share figure deserves its own treatment. With $173.6 million in free cash flow generated in 2025 against 32.6 million diluted shares, an owner's FCF per share was approximately $5.32 for the year. At the current share price of approximately $170, that translates to a free cash flow yield of roughly 3.1%. That is not a cheap entry point by absolute standards, but it is a yield being produced by a business that has grown its FCF per share from approximately $3.21 in 2016 to $5.32 in 2025, a compound annual growth rate of approximately 5.8%, without meaningful dilution and without relying on tax dynamics to artificially lift the result. That growth rate is honest, and it sets the baseline for what an owner is actually underwriting at today's price.

The dividend record adds another dimension. On December 9, 2025, the board declared an annual dividend of $0.96 per share, a 10.3% increase over the prior year's payment. That marked the 17th consecutive annual dividend increase. A streak of that length is not coincidental. It reflects a management team that has set the dividend only when it was confident the business could sustain and grow it, year after year, through multiple industrial cycles, through the pandemic disruption that briefly compressed Specialty Products revenue, and through the inflationary input cost environment of 2022 and 2023. The discipline behind that streak is more informative than the dividend yield itself, which at $0.96 against a $170 share price sits below 0.6%, meaningful not as income, but as a signal of management's confidence in the earnings base.

Taken together, the per-share picture here is one of genuine, unmanipulated compounding. Diluted share count flat to declining. Tax rate stable. FCF conversion consistently above 100% of net income. Earnings growth driven by operating leverage and volume expansion rather than financial engineering. For an owner evaluating whether the headline growth numbers reflect reality, the answer at Balchem is yes, and that is not the automatic conclusion it might appear to be.

What the Market Is Paying and What It Is Getting

At a share price of approximately $170.91 as of March 11, 2026, the market is pricing Balchem at roughly 33 times its 2025 adjusted earnings of $5.15 per share. On a free cash flow basis, the entry yield is 3.1% against the $5.32 in FCF per share the business generated last year. Neither of those figures looks inexpensive in isolation. The investment question is whether they are inexpensive relative to what this business actually is, and what a buyer at today's price is realistically underwriting over the next decade.

Operating margin shows whether Balchem's formulation-embedded economics actually translate into superior profitability versus peers who compete in similar specialty ingredients markets, they do, by a margin of 7 to 12 percentage points. FCF yield shows what an owner actually receives today for the price paid, a figure that is relevant in isolation but more meaningful when compared against the quality of the cash being generated. EV/EBITDA anchors the valuation in enterprise terms, removing the effect of capital structure differences across the three businesses.

Sensient Technologies is the closest structural comparison. It serves the food, pharmaceutical, and personal care markets with specialty flavors and colors, operates with formulation-service relationships, and competes in categories where switching friction exists. Yet the company generated only $38.4 million in free cash flow for the full year 2025, the result of $89.4 million in capital expenditures required to fund its heavy investment in natural color conversion capacity, a multi-year program management expects to weigh on results through at least 2027. The FCF yield near 1% is not a bargain. It reflects a business in investment mode, funded with net debt at 2.3 times EBITDA. Sensient may deliver strong returns from here if its natural color strategy succeeds, but an owner today is bearing the full weight of that investment risk. The EV/EBITDA gap between the two companies, 20.3 times for Balchem versus 15.3 times for Sensient, is partially explained by Balchem's structurally cleaner cash generation and the absence of a multi-year capex cycle. Paying a 33% premium in EV/EBITDA terms for a business that converts its EBITDA to free cash flow at more than four times the rate is not automatically excessive.

Innospec presents a different contrast. It trades at 7.8 times EBITDA, carries net cash, and offers a 3.7% FCF yield, figures that look superficially cheaper than Balchem on every headline metric. But Innospec's full-year 2025 adjusted EPS of $5.27 was actually down from $5.92 the prior year. Revenue declined 4%. Adjusted EBITDA fell 10%. The Fuel Specialties division remains its most profitable segment but is structurally exposed to the long-term transition away from fossil fuels, a headwind that has no equivalent at Balchem. An owner buying Innospec at today's price is paying 7.8 times EBITDA for a business whose earnings are contracting and whose most profitable segment faces secular pressure. An owner buying Balchem at 20.3 times EBITDA is paying for a business whose earnings grew 17.8% in 2025 and whose three segments each grew simultaneously. The absolute cheapness of Innospec is priced for a reason.

The return arithmetic for Balchem is more straightforward than either peer. At a 3.1% FCF yield as the starting point, and with a verified 10-year FCF per share CAGR of approximately 10.3%, an owner underwriting continuation of that trajectory at today's price is looking at a total annual return in the range of 13%, before any change in the multiple. That figure requires the 2022 acquisition-driven dip in FCF to not repeat in a structurally damaging way, which the 2023-to-2025 recovery argues it has not. A more conservative assumption, FCF per share compounding at 6 to 8% annually, consistent with analyst consensus and the company's own guidance of continued above-market growth, produces a total return in the 9% to 11% range. That is the honest underwriting at today's price for an owner who believes the formulation-embedded economics are durable but does not model any multiple re-rating.

One recent development deserves direct attention. The current global trade environment, characterized by elevated US tariff threats and retaliatory measures, has created significant operational uncertainty for companies with international supply chains. The CEO acknowledged this explicitly in the Q1 2025 earnings release, describing Balchem as focused on remaining "nimble and flexible" through market volatility. Importantly, the structural buffer here is real: approximately 85% of Balchem's products are manufactured and sold within the same region, meaning US volumes are largely produced domestically and EU volumes in the EU. This intra-regional model, built over decades of deliberate operational design, provides a degree of tariff insulation that most specialty chemicals peers cannot match at the same scale. It does not eliminate currency risk or input cost volatility, but it removes the most acute exposure, the cross-border shipment of finished goods subject to new import duties, that is currently repricing risk across the broader industrial and chemicals sector.

The $36 million microencapsulation capacity expansion underway at the company's Orange County, New York facility introduces a modest near-term weight on FCF as construction and commissioning costs flow through capital expenditures over the next 12 to 18 months. This is not a concern, but it is worth naming honestly: FCF conversion will likely be slightly below the 105% ratio seen in 2025 for at least one year as the new facility ramps. An owner who understands this will not misread a temporary FCF dip as a structural deterioration. An owner who does not understand it might.

Taken together, the valuation case rests not on cheapness but on honest categorization. Balchem is priced at a premium to most specialty chemicals peers because its economics deserve a premium. The question is whether the premium is justified, and the peer comparison argues it is, specifically because the margin gap, the FCF conversion gap, and the per-share compounding gap between Balchem and the companies it is implicitly grouped with are not small. At today's price, an owner is paying 20.3 times EBITDA for a business generating 20.2% operating margins with 0.3 times leverage and a decade of clean per-share compounding. That is not a value trap masquerading as quality. Nor is it a bargain. It is a business priced at a level that requires the formulation-embedded economics to continue performing. And for anyone who has spent time understanding why those economics hold, that requirement is not the same as a leap of faith.

Smart Money's Read on Balchem

The ownership register for Balchem does not tell a single story, it tells two, and the tension between them is worth examining carefully before drawing conclusions about what sophisticated capital is communicating about this business. The 4.9 million shares held by BlackRock, valued at $753 million at the time of the last filing, representing by far the largest institutional position visible. BlackRock's average buy price of $96.91, sitting 74.4% below today's price, reflects a position built over many years, added to consistently, and never meaningfully reduced. This is not a thematic trade or a recent discovery. It is the kind of long, uninterrupted accumulation that index and quality-factor strategies execute in businesses they have concluded deserve a permanent seat in broadly diversified portfolios. BlackRock's presence at this scale is a statement about durability, not about a specific catalyst.

Dimensional Fund Advisors tells a nearly identical story. With 598,000 shares worth $91.7 million and an average buy price of $62.46, implying an unrealized gain of approximately 170%, Dimensional has owned this business through multiple cycles and continued adding. The modest 3.26% increase in the most recent quarter (+18,900 shares) confirms this is an active, maintained position, not a legacy holding left unmanaged. Dimensional builds exposure to companies that score consistently on quality, profitability, and return-on-capital screens. The fact that Balchem has remained in that portfolio long enough to generate a 170% gain on cost is itself analytical evidence: a business whose fundamentals have deteriorated does not stay in a Dimensional portfolio at scale.

The more active end of the register produces a sharper signal. Point72 Asset Management, led by Steven Cohen (Trades, Portfolio), increased its position by nearly 396% in the most recent quarter, adding 51,500 shares to reach 64,500 shares valued at $9.9 million. This is a new-size conviction position, not a routine trim or addition. Point72 is not a passive quality allocator; it runs concentrated, high-conviction strategies across fundamental and quantitative approaches. A near-five-fold increase in a position suggests a specific thesis, and given the timing, Q4 2025, immediately after Balchem reported record full-year results, the most plausible read is that Point72 saw the combination of record earnings, a 25% stock pullback from recent highs, and clean per-share economics as a dislocation worth acting on decisively.

Tudor Investment, Paul Tudor Jones (Trades, Portfolio)'s firm, added 81.47% to its position in the same period (+17,300 shares), reaching 38,400 shares at an average buy price of $157.71. Tudor is a macro and multi-strategy manager whose equity positions tend to reflect either factor exposure or specific value signals. The timing and the magnitude of the addition, again in Q4 2025, align with the same thesis.

The other side of the ledger requires equal honesty. Grantham Mayo Van Otterloo, Jeremy Grantham (Trades, Portfolio)'s firm, reduced its position by 41.03%, cutting 146,000 shares while still holding 210,000. Renaissance Technologies (Trades, Portfolio) reduced by 37.04%, cutting 89,500 shares while maintaining 152,000. Two Sigma trimmed modestly. These are not exits, the positions remain meaningful, but they are reductions from firms whose models appear to have signaled at least partial profit-taking after a period of strong appreciation. Grantham's firm in particular has a well-documented tendency to reduce in businesses it considers moderately or fully valued relative to intrinsic worth; a 41% reduction is not a rout, but it is not a casual trim either.

What does the collective picture say to a potential long-term owner?

The register shows a business that has earned its place in long-duration, quality-oriented portfolios while simultaneously attracting fresh conviction from multi-strategy managers who see a specific opportunity at current prices. The reductions from quant and value managers are real, but the continuity of Dimensional's holding since an average cost of $62 and BlackRock's position built from below $97 suggests that investors with the longest time horizons have not concluded the investment case is exhausted. The split between those trimming and those adding likely reflects the same underlying fact from different angles: this is a business that has compounded to a price where opinions about near-term value diverge, even while the underlying quality is not in dispute.

What Could Go Wrong

The investment case for Balchem rests on three interconnected assumptions: that the formulation-embedded economics in Human Nutrition and Health continue to generate the growth rates seen in 2024 and 2025, that the Specialty Products segment operates without material regulatory disruption to its ethylene oxide business, and that the Animal Nutrition segment's newly protected position in Europe translates into durable improvement rather than temporary relief. Each of those assumptions deserves honest interrogation, because the valuation at 20 times EBITDA leaves limited room for any of them to fail.

The most operationally complex risk sits inside Specialty Products. Balchem's subsidiary BCP Ingredients manufactures and repackages ethylene oxide at its Verona, Missouri facility, and the regulatory history of that site has been neither quiet nor resolved. EPA Region 7 has been actively engaged with the facility since 2016, when the agency updated its cancer risk values for ethylene oxide and subsequently identified elevated cancer risk levels in communities near certain industrial emitters including BCP. A 2022 accidental release of ethylene oxide at the Verona facility triggered a compliance inspection. Stack tests conducted in February 2023 raised questions about emissions during railcar unloading that required corrective action. In January 2025, the EPA finalized an interim registration review decision for ethylene oxide that introduced a suite of new mitigation measures for workers and surrounding communities at sterilization facilities. The current Trump administration EPA moved in March 2025 to reconsider the more stringent Biden-era 2024 NESHAP emissions standards for commercial sterilizers, arguing they threatened the domestic medical device supply chain, a reconsideration that provides near-term operational relief. But that relief is contingent on the current regulatory posture holding. A future administration, or a further evolution in the EPA's risk assessments, could reintroduce compliance requirements that raise operating costs, restrict throughput, or create legal exposure at facilities where community health litigation is already active. Balchem disclosed in its 2024 10-K that certain claims against the company in Puerto Rico litigation related to ethylene oxide exposure were recommended for dismissal by a magistrate judge, but the litigation itself has not been fully resolved. For a segment that earned $42.9 million in operating earnings in 2025 on $141 million in revenue, a sustained compliance-driven cost increase or facility restriction would meaningfully affect a business that currently runs at a 30% operating margin. That margin is not guaranteed by the product economics alone. It is also supported by an operating environment that has been more permissive than it may permanently remain.

The second risk is more arithmetic than operational, but equally important. The current valuation at approximately 33 times adjusted earnings requires the business to continue delivering the kind of earnings growth it produced in 2025. Adjusted EPS grew 17.8% that year. Human Nutrition and Health, which drives more than 60% of revenue and the majority of operating earnings, grew sales 12.7% in Q4 and approximately 9.9% for the full year 2025. If that segment's growth rate normalizes toward the mid-single digits, driven by a slower-growing end market, pricing that has reached its natural ceiling with established customers, or margin pressure from the manufacturing input costs that management referenced in both Q3 and Q4 earnings releases, the total company earnings growth would compress sharply. At a 3.1% FCF yield and a growth assumption of 5% rather than 10%, the implied total return drops to the 8% range, which is still acceptable but no longer the clear premium over opportunity cost that the current case presents. The new microencapsulation facility under construction in Orange County, New York, is designed to expand capacity and support continued growth in the nutrition segment, but it will not contribute meaningfully to revenue until 2027 at the earliest, and construction-phase capital expenditures will weigh on FCF conversion in the interim. An owner needs to carry that timing mismatch through the holding period without misreading it as a fundamental deterioration.

The third risk is the most bounded but also the most frequently underappreciated. The EU anti-dumping duties on Chinese choline chloride, effective for five years beginning December 2025, provide structural protection for Balchem's European Animal Nutrition operations, but they do not protect the US market, where choline chloride pricing remains sensitive to lower-cost competition. Balchem's own 10-K states that success in the commodity-oriented choline chloride marketplace is highly dependent on maintaining product quality and customer service rather than on proprietary technology or regulatory barriers. That is an honest disclosure. In practical terms, it means the Animal Nutrition segment's improvement in 2025, operating earnings of $18.7 million versus $14.0 million in 2024, a 33% increase, reflects a combination of volume growth, the early benefit of improved EU pricing, and favorable mix. The sustainability of that improvement in the US market will depend on pricing discipline that the company does not fully control. If Chinese producers redirect volumes toward the US in response to EU exclusion, the competitive pressure that the EU duties removed from the European business could materialize in the North American market instead. That is not a certain outcome, but it is a logical one that any careful owner should account for when assessing the durability of the Animal Nutrition segment's recovery.

None of these risks break the investment thesis. They define where the thesis can be stress-tested, and stress-testing is the appropriate response to a business trading at a premium multiple with record results just reported. The case for owning Balchem rests on formulation-embedded economics that are genuinely differentiated, but differentiated economics priced at 33 times earnings require everything that is currently working to keep working.

The Ingredient the Market Keeps Mislabeling

The case for owning Balchem does not rest on a market correction. It rests on the business continuing to do what it has done without interruption across multiple cycles, compound per-share value, grow the dividend, and generate free cash flow from formulation relationships that deepen rather than erode over time. That is a specific kind of promise, and the historical record backs it precisely. Seventeen consecutive annual dividend increases. A beta of 0.37 that confirms the stock has historically absorbed less than half of the broader market's volatility. No full-year losses across a history that stretches back through the 2008 financial crisis, the 2015 industrial downturn, and the supply chain disruptions of the early 2020s. For an investor whose goal is to reduce portfolio risk without abandoning return potential entirely, this combination of economic resilience and market-price stability is rare and genuinely valuable.

The return math at today's price is honest rather than exciting. A 3.1% starting FCF yield, combined with the mid-to-high single-digit annual growth rate the business has sustained through its formulation-embedded customer relationships and disciplined capital allocation, produces a total annual return in the high single to low double digits. That is not extraordinary. It is the dependable compounding that a high-quality specialty ingredients platform offers when purchased at a fair price, solid enough to compound meaningfully over a five-year horizon, conservative enough not to require anything unusual to go right.

What makes the current moment worth examining specifically is the price. After trading above its recent highs earlier in the year, the stock has pulled back approximately 10% over the past month, not because the business deteriorated, but because broader market anxiety moved the price independently of the underlying economics. The 2025 results were records. The EU anti-dumping protections on Chinese choline chloride are intact. The Orange County microencapsulation expansion is underway. Periodic dislocations like this one are the moments when Balchem's defensive profile becomes its most attractive, the formulation lock-in, the balance sheet at 0.3 times net debt to EBITDA, and the 17-year dividend track record remain entirely unchanged. For a patient owner, that is the entry point the business rarely offers.

Balchem is set to report Q1 2026 results in late April, and that release will offer the first read on whether the growth momentum that defined 2025 has carried into the new year. The numbers most worth watching are not the headline revenue figure but the internals that confirm or challenge the investment thesis. Human Nutrition and Health segment growth is the primary signal, the 12.7% Q4 2025 increase set a high baseline, and any meaningful deceleration toward the mid-single digits would prompt a reassessment of the valuation. Gross margin is the second dimension to monitor, given the modest compression from 29.1% in 2024 to 28.3% in 2025 that management attributed to input cost pressures; stabilization or improvement here would confirm that the 2025 margin profile was a temporary headwind rather than a structural shift. FCF conversion will also bear watching as the Orange County microencapsulation facility moves further into construction: capital expenditures will likely run above the $53.7 million reported for 2025 over the next 12 to 18 months, temporarily compressing the FCF yield from today's 3.1% entry point. An owner who has read the thesis carefully will not mistake that compression for deterioration, but the Q1 release will clarify the trajectory.

For investors who think about their choices across the universe of high-quality, entrenched compounders, Balchem presents a specific and deliberately different trade-off. Moody's carries a beta of 0.97 and has grown adjusted EPS at roughly 20% annually over the past three years, a powerful compounder, but one that moves nearly in lockstep with the broader market and can swing sharply when expectations for financial data revenues shift. Alphabet carries a beta of approximately 1.13 and delivered a five-year EPS CAGR of close to 30%, exceptional growth, but a stock that amplifies market moves rather than absorbing them. Visa sits between the two, with a beta of approximately 0.58 and a five-year EPS CAGR of roughly 16%. Each of these businesses is legitimately excellent. Each earns the premium the market places on it. The point is not that Balchem is better than any of them. The point is that Balchem, with a beta of 0.37 and a five-year adjusted EPS CAGR of approximately 12%, occupies a specific position on the spectrum: lower price sensitivity to changes in short-term expectations, lower amplification of market volatility, and steady mid-double-digit compounding from relationships that deepen over time rather than depending on advertising cycles, credit market volumes, or payment network transaction growth. For an investor who has decided that the near-term return from lower-volatility compounding is an acceptable trade against higher-growth alternatives, Balchem is one of the cleaner expressions of that choice available at a reasonable entry price.

data.title: India's RBL Bank's quarterly profit jumps on solid loan growth
data.description:
India's RBL Bank reported a three-fold jump in fourth-quarter profit on Saturday, helped by strong loan growth.

The private lender posted a standalone net profit of 2.30 billion Indian rupees ($24.41 million) for the quarter ended March 31, compared with 687 million rupees a year earlier.

After months of slower growth, Indian lenders saw a pick-up in credit demand in the second half of the financial year, aided by consumption tax cuts and a recovery in corporate loans.

RBL's loans grew 23% in its fourth quarter from a year earlier, while deposits rose 25%.

Net interest income - the difference between interest earned on advances and paid on deposits - grew 7% to 16.71 billion rupees.

Earlier this month, India's central bank approved Emirates NBD Bank's proposal to ​acquire a majority stake in RBL Bank, giving a key regulatory clearance for one of the largest cross-border deals in India's financial sector.

Last October, Emirates NBD announced plans to acquire a 60% stake in the Indian lender for $3 billion.

RBL Bank will be classified as a ‌foreign ⁠bank subsidiary with Emirates NBD as its parent, the Reserve Bank of India said in its approval, which is valid for a year.

The bank's gross bad loans as a percentage of total loans improved to 1.45%, as of the end of March, from 1.88% in the previous quarter.

Provisions to cover potential bad loans dropped 13.7% to 6.78 billion rupees.

($1 = 94.2400 Indian rupees)

data.title: Shipping Group Says US, Iran Ship Seizures Violate International Law: Report
data.description:
Key points:
  • A global shipping body has denounced the United States and Iran for the seizure of commercial vessels.
  • The warning comes as US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner arrive in Islamabad for indirect talks with Iran, with Pakistan as a mediator.
  • Iran's Strait of Hormuz closure, demands for its nuclear program, sanctions relief, and rising maritime tensions affecting global trade remain unresolved.

As tensions between the two countries remain high ahead of indirect talks in Pakistan, the tit-for-tat seizure of commercial vessels by the United States and Iran has been deemed illegal under international law on Saturday.

John Stawpert, Principal Director Marine at the International Chamber of Shipping (ICS), has condemned the actions and called for the immediate release of detained crews, telling that seafarers should be allowed to operate “freely and without persecution”.

“All these people are doing is transporting trade. And really, we can’t have a situation where ships are being seized, ultimately for political ends, to prove a political point,” Stawpert said. “These are innocent seafarers, and they should be allowed to go about their jobs without fear of, essentially, imprisonment.”

Steve Witkoff And Jared Kushner In Islamabad

The warning comes as US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner travel to Islamabad for talks that include Iran, though Tehran has denied any direct meeting with US officials, saying its positions will be conveyed via Pakistani intermediaries.

According to the report, Iran's Foreign Minister, Abbas Araghchi, arrived in the capital on a regional tour, and Pakistani mediators are “cautiously optimistic.” The content of the talks, however, remains under media blackout, but key issues remain unresolved, including Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital global shipping route. Last week, Iran described the US Navy’s blockade of Iranian ports as " piracy " and shut the waterway.

The U.S. Global Sea to Sky Cargo ETF (SEA) rose 0.48% to about $17.23 at Friday’s close, extending gains slightly in after-hours trading. The ETF showed a steady upward trend during the session. On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around SEA remained in the ‘extremely bullish’ zone, with ‘extremely high’ chatter levels over the past day.

Additional issues include Iran's ballistic missile program, sanctions relief, frozen assets, and potential war reparations. According to former US President Donald Trump, any agreement must necessitate Iran's surrender of enriched uranium and the reopening of the Strait.

Concerns within the global shipping industry have been exacerbated by the escalation at sea, which includes reciprocal ship seizures. The industry cautions that the safety of civilian crews and international trade may be at risk due to ongoing disruptions.

Growing Military Dissent

These developments come on the heels of Mike Prysner, Executive Director of the Center on Conscience & War (CCW) in Washington, a former Iraq war veteran, reportedly saying that while the U.S. hasn't had a draft since 1973, a recent measure in the defense budget for the U.S. could automatically register males aged 18-25 for the Selective Service. Now, military veterans warn current service members against repeating past mistakes seen during the Vietnam War in the current Iran-US conflict.

The Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) closed lower on the session, falling 1.83% to around $67.57, though it recovered modestly in after-hours trading. The ARK Space & Defense ETF (ARKX) also edged lower on Friday, slipping 0.45% to approximately $32.97. On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around SHLD stock and ARKX stock remained in the ‘bullish’ zone while chatter around them remained at ‘high’ levels over the past day.

data.title: RBL Bank Q4: Profit jumps 234%; asset quality strengthens, advances grow 23%
data.description:
RBL Bank reported a strong March quarter performance on Saturday, April 25, with net profit surging 234% year-on-year to ₹230 crore, as improved asset quality.

Net interest income (NII) rose 7% YoY and 1% sequentially to ₹1,671 crore, while net interest margin (NIM) stood at 4.41%. Other income grew 7% YoY to ₹1,069 crore, supported by a 9% rise in core fee income to ₹1,057 crore.

Operating profit increased 11% YoY to ₹955 crore, even as operating expenses rose 5% to ₹1,785 crore. The cost-to-income ratio improved to 65.1% from 66.3% in the previous quarter.

On the balance sheet front, net advances grew 23% YoY to ₹1.14 lakh crore, led by strong traction in retail and wholesale segments. Retail advances rose 20% YoY to ₹67,119 crore, while wholesale advances grew 28% to ₹47,112 crore. Total deposits increased 25% YoY to ₹1.39 lakh crore, with CASA deposits rising 23% to ₹46,723 crore.

Asset quality improved during the quarter, with gross non-performing assets (GNPA) declining 43 basis points sequentially to 1.45%, while net NPA eased to 0.39%. Provision coverage ratio stood strong at 94.9%.

For FY26, net profit rose 18% YoY to ₹822 crore. The bank proposed a dividend of ₹1 per share. NII for the year declined 2% to ₹6,360 crore, while operating profit stood at ₹3,299 crore.

Shares of the company ended nearly 3% higher at ₹321.65 on Friday, ahead of the March quarter earnings results. The stock has surged over 71% in the last one year.

data.title: What It's Like to Retire in America After a Divorce — WSJ
data.description:
By Veronica Dagher and Anne Tergesen | Design by Annie Ng

This week, we're bringing you stories about the financial impact of divorce.

For many Americans, their golden years are being rewritten by divorce.

While national divorce rates have hit historic lows for younger generations, the rate for older Americans has climbed. People 50 or older now make up nearly 40% of the divorcing population, up from just 8% in 1990, according to research from Bowling Green State University.

This late-stage split often upends retirement plans, forcing people to navigate expenses with halved 401(k)s and little time to shore up their savings. That's all while facing the daunting task of rebuilding their lives.

Yet many end up finding a sense of freedom in their newfound independence. We spoke with four retirees about how they successfully navigated life after divorce.

Albert Ferreira

Lincoln, Calif. | Age: 71

Annual Spending: $66,000

Albert Ferreira got married when he was 47 — a late start that he believes contributed to his divorce 20 years later. "I was set in my ways and probably should have stayed single," says Ferreira, now 71.

The financial and emotional reset after the split was significant. The divorce settlement required the sale of the family home and he traded a mortgage rate under 4% for one closer to 5.5%. He used $350,000 of the sale proceeds to buy into a 55+ community in Lincoln, Calif.

It was more than two hours away from his old home, and Ferreira didn't know anyone.

Since his previous social circle was largely tied to his ex-wife's family, he focused on rebuilding his life. He now has a weekly lunch with single and retired male neighbors, golfs, and has taken up pickleball. His dog, which he kept in the divorce, has become a social bridge in the community.

Ferreira retired at 62 following a stroke, when he was still married. He credits his ex-wife with helping nurse him back to health and maintains a friendly relationship with her. He receives $4,500 a month from a pension and Social Security and has $175,000 in retirement accounts and about $120,000 in savings.

He spends about $5,500 a month, most of which goes toward his mortgage and entertainment. He's working on spending less so he doesn't have to dip into savings each month. He recently started buying fruits and vegetables from a farmers market and is cooking more at home.

Ferreira recently spent about $2,000 to update his will, healthcare proxy and account beneficiaries, naming his son as his primary representative. As the bad feelings about his divorce have faded, he also named his ex-wife as his backup proxy to ensure a trusted person is available to step in if needed.

"I try not to ruminate on the past but look to the future," he said.

Debi Petriscak

Birmingham, Ala. | Age: 71

Annual spending: $100,000

Debi Petriscak was 64 when her marriage ended after 38 years. The separation from the father of her four children upended the retirement she had been envisioning.

As part of the settlement, Petriscak, now 71, took ownership of the family home in Calabasas, Calif. But maintenance costs were high, and the memories made living in the house painful.

When she sold it about four years later, she was hit with a nearly $200,000 tax bill. Because she was filing as a single person, her capital gains tax exclusion dropped to $250,000 from $500,000. This "single penalty" resulted in a substantial tax hit that she could have avoided had the home been sold soon after the divorce.

The sting didn't end there. Because the house sale drove up her reported income for the year, she was hit with an IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) surcharge, a cost that caused her Medicare Part B and D premiums to more than double.

Seeking a fresh start, Petriscak retired from her career as a geriatric nurse practitioner and left California. She moved to Birmingham, Ala., to be near some of her adult children.

"Being closer to my family saved me," she says.

Using some of the house proceeds, she paid $640,000 in cash for her new home, which is perfect for hosting the large holiday gatherings she loves. She also feels more secure in Alabama, where the cost of living is lower.

She has a monthly income of around $10,000 including Social Security and a pension, around $80,000 in cash and $1.3 million in retirement accounts. She spends about $100,000 a year, with a significant amount going to travel.

One of her biggest adjustments was the shift to making financial decisions on her own. Losing a lifelong sounding board was daunting. To avoid feeling like a burden to her children, she hired a financial adviser.

She has joined a quilting club, found a church community and has a dedicated exercise routine. More importantly, she's seeing all the places she has wanted to visit. After meeting a widow on a singles' trip, the two have traveled as far as Africa together. She also takes a trip abroad every year with her sister. Southern Italy is next on their list.

"I'm going to travel to my entire bucket list," she says.

Mark Sutton

Woodbury, Minn. | Age: 74

Annual spending: $240,000

As Mark Sutton was nearing retirement over a decade ago, his wife of 23 years filed for divorce.

"It was a brutal shock," said Sutton, now 74.

On top of the emotional blow, the divorce in 2014 slashed his net worth. He and his former wife divided his pension and his $1.1 million 401(k). He also paid her for her share of the value of their home, which he still lives in.

To make up for the financial setback, Sutton postponed his retirement from age 65 to 70.

At work, the former 3M executive started socializing with colleagues who were also going through divorces. They would meet up for dinner or drinks to compare notes on the legal process, or just engage in "a little bit of emotional venting," he said.

Sutton also found comfort in yoga and cycling, and eventually saw the silver lining in being single. He now spends four months a year in his native New Zealand, where his 95-year-old mother and three of his siblings live.

His 401(k) is now at $2.3 million. That's due to both the rising stock market and the additional years he worked, in which he saved the maximum amount each year, including the extra catch-up contributions people 50 or older can make.

Sutton's retirement income is $240,000 a year after taxes. That includes the roughly $105,000 he withdraws annually from the 401(k), as well as $3,700 a month in Social Security and a $7,800 pension.

A big chunk of his spending goes to travel, including to New Zealand, and to see his two sons — both professional musicians — perform. "These are my go-go years," he said.

He gives his sons about $30,000 a year. One is temporarily living with him, an arrangement he enjoys.

Sutton loves his pool and garage, which houses his Porsche 928. But the Formula One fan plans to eventually downsize to reduce his costs. His home insurance, property taxes and mortgage cost around $59,700 a year. To buy his former wife's portion of the house, Sutton refinanced the mortgage and owes $300,000 on an adjustable-rate mortgage that's now charging 6.75%.

In the end, Sutton said his post-divorce life came together better than he had expected.

"I had a rich career and a great life. I feel very fortunate. Now I am free to travel and do the things I love," he said.

Lynn Meinor

Salt Lake City & Tucson, Ariz. | Age: 70

Annual spending: $86,000

When Lynn Meinor realized she might be heading for a divorce three decades ago, she made a key decision with her retirement in mind.

Instead of taking a job in the private sector, she joined the Utah Department of Health & Human Services. A big draw was the state's pension.

"It was a relief to know I'd have that income to rely on," she said.

The Salt Lake City resident struggled to support her two young daughters on her paycheck and child support. Meinor's college friends, who she considers family, offered a safety net. They helped each other with childcare, and one loaned her money for her daughters' tuition and then forgave the loan.

On her father's advice, Meinor always contributed something to her 401(k)-like plan, no matter how small.

She remarried in 2000 and ramped up her retirement savings, to about 20% of her pay. Her second husband paid the bills, including their mortgage.

Meinor loved her job in public health, and had been developing a program for Alzheimer's and related dementias. Then the pandemic hit, and she got reassigned to working with long-term-care facilities, which were hit hard by Covid-19.

Burned out by 16-hour workdays, she retired earlier than planned in late 2020.

In 2023, Meinor and her second husband divorced. He kept one of their two houses. She has the other, worth about $700,000, which she rents to her daughter. She and her ex each retained their own savings; Meinor's consists of a $225,000 retirement account and her pension.

Two years ago, Meinor withdrew $70,000 from her retirement account to buy a $340,000 condo in Tucson. The climate "is conducive to my lifestyle and aging well," said Meinor, a breast cancer survivor.

She's exercising more than ever, for enjoyment and because research shows it helps prevent illnesses, including dementia.

Meinor lives on a $3,500 monthly pension, $2,200 in Social Security and $1,500 in rental income. Her mortgage is $2,000 a month. She spends about $300 on HOA fees, $400 on food and $300 on utilities.

A year ago, Meinor married a third time. The couple divide their time between Tucson and his home in Salt Lake City. They share expenses but keep their assets separate, for their respective children to inherit.

Meinor said her mother taught her the benefit of a positive mental attitude. She adds some wisdom of her own: "It's very important as you age to maintain close personal relationships."

data.title: Internships Are More Crucial Than Ever — and Even Harder to Find — WSJ
data.description:
By Oyin Adedoyin and Ray A. Smith

For students prepping for life after college, it's an agonizing challenge: Internships are becoming more critical to landing that first job — yet are getting harder to come by.

Even as graduate hiring shows early glimmers of a rebound, summer internship opportunities appear to be getting scarcer. Internship postings on early-career job platform Handshake were down 16% earlier this year, continuing a decline since 2023, its data show. On jobs site Indeed, postings are slightly up from 2025's sluggish internship season but well below previous years.

Competition for those internships, meanwhile, is only getting more intense: Internships posted on Handshake during the 2024-25 academic year drew an average of 109 applicants, up from 62 the year prior and more than double the number in 2023.

Much of the intensifying scramble has to do with the ever-higher stakes of landing a solid job out of college, a feat that's grown more difficult as employers slow hiring and AI reshapes many entry-level roles.

With unemployment for recent graduates this past year at decade highs — excluding the early pandemic — some are vying for internships while still hunting for full-time jobs. So are more college freshmen and sophomores, in the hopes of improving their own hiring chances later on, campus career counselors say.

"The reality is that for internships, students have got to get in the process very early," said Nicole van den Heuvel, executive director of Rice University's career-development center. "When we talk to freshmen during their orientation week, we're in front of the students telling them, 'You've got to start thinking about your career in tandem with your academic experience.' It's more important now."

Chloey Cho, a 19-year-old wrapping up her freshman year at the University of California, Los Angeles, has already completed four internships alongside her studies, two in finance and two in marketing. Her goal: to maximize her chances of getting a full-time role after school.

But the musicology and economics major said she has noticed an ominous trend: She learned this month that one of the internship programs that typically recruits from the on-campus investing and trading club she belongs to was no longer taking them because the firm now relied on AI to do intern-level tasks.

"So they won't be needing interns anymore," she said, adding that she has noticed a similar trend among other finance firms. "I was a bit demotivated."

Some employers, like McKinsey, say they are boosting the size of their intern classes this year. Yet others have canceled or cut them back. Workplace technology company Paycom Software, which laid off more than 500 staff last fall and automated some back-office roles, recently notified interns recruited to work there this summer that it was cutting their internships, too, students and professors say. The company didn't respond to requests for comment.

Some students are coming up with alternatives. Nathan Goldstein, a 20-year-old electrical-engineering major at Union College, in Schenectady, N.Y., began his internship search four months sooner than last year after seeing friends struggle to find one.

Meanwhile, he helped start a campus group to help classmates to get networking and unpaid work experience on projects for nonprofits and small businesses. He expected maybe six students to show up to the first meeting. Instead, 30 attended. Even more emailed afterward to find out how they could line up some work experience, he said.

"It's just a really tough internship market to get into," said Goldstein, who said he hopes to finalize his own internship plans soon.

Securing even one internship during college significantly improves the odds of landing a college-level job upon graduation, a growing body of research shows. In a ZipRecruiter survey of 3,000 soon-to-be and recent graduates released this month, those who had had internships were more likely to land a full-time job offer before graduation. They were also more likely to describe those jobs as real career steppingstones.

The share of recent college graduates who are underemployed — meaning, they are in jobs that don't require a college degree — stood at 42.5% as of the end of 2025, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

Companies, meanwhile, are hiring more of the interns they do select for full-time roles later. The average rate at which employers converted interns into full-time hires in 2025 reached a five-year high of 63%, according to a recent survey of 284 employers by the National Association of Colleges and Employers. The survey found their intern hiring was up 3.9% this year, after falling by nearly the same rate last year.

They want "to ensure upfront they have candidates who have proven themselves rather than having a large pool of candidates who haven't tested their skills, " particularly in this economic environment, said Shawn VanDerziel, NACE's chief executive.

Emil Michael, a 20-year-old finance and logistics management major at Ohio State University, is currently interning at a healthcare startup. But he is still on the hunt for one this summer, and has even applied for internships for the summer of 2027, too.

He has his summer job from last year, as a dock hand for a ferry, to fall back on, he said, but that's not quite the same.

"It's not really business related so the experience wouldn't be as relevant," he said.

Write to Oyin Adedoyin at oyin.adedoyin@wsj.com and Ray A. Smith at Ray.Smith@wsj.com
